question: In the notes, what is a predicate?

options.text: An operator like `+` or `&&` used inside expressions.
options.feedback: Operators are symbols; predicates are full expressions yielding boolean values.

options.text: A global constant written in uppercase to avoid mutation.
options.feedback: Uppercase naming is convention, but predicate definition concerns evaluation result.

options.text: An expression that evaluates to either true or false.
options.feedback: Correct: predicates produce boolean outcomes used by conditional logic.

options.text: A function signature listing parameter and return data types.
options.feedback: Signatures describe contracts; they do not themselves evaluate boolean outcomes.

whyQuestion: Why are predicates central when designing function logic?

whyOptions.text: Because predicates eliminate boundary cases by ignoring edge inputs.
whyOptions.feedback: Boundary cases still exist and must be tested explicitly in design.

whyOptions.text: Because predicates replace the need for tests in HTDF workflow.
whyOptions.feedback: Tests remain required; predicates only define decision conditions within logic.

whyOptions.text: Because they drive branching decisions and filtering based on explicit conditions.
whyOptions.feedback: Exactly: reliable condition checks control execution paths and expected outcomes.

whyOptions.text: Because predicates automatically convert all function outputs to constants.
whyOptions.feedback: Predicates evaluate conditions; they do not rewrite output mutability semantics.
